skill.name: azure-ai-anomalydetector-java
skill.description: Build Java applications that use Azure AI Anomaly Detector for univariate and multivariate time-series monitoring.
skill.summary: Create Java time-series anomaly detection workflows with Azure AI Anomaly Detector.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static command-execution findings are false positives caused by Markdown code fences and Java examples, not executable Ruby or shell backticks. The skill documents legitimate Azure SDK network calls and environment variable credential loading, with placeholder SAS-token URLs that require normal secret-handling caution. No prompt injection, exfiltration intent, or malicious behavior was found in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: Credential and Endpoint Configuration Examples
low_findings.description: The guide reads Azure endpoint and API key values from environment variables and shows a placeholder API key assignment. This is a legitimate SDK pattern, but users must avoid logging or committing real secret values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines explicitly show Azure credential configuration. The values are placeholders or environment variables, so this is a documentation caution rather than malicious credential access.

low_findings.title: Placeholder Network Data Sources With SAS Token Text
low_findings.description: The multivariate examples use Azure Blob Storage URLs containing placeholder SAS-token text. This is expected for Azure Anomaly Detector training and inference, but users should treat real SAS URLs as secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are directly present and are tied to Azure Blob Storage data inputs. They are placeholders and align with the SDK workflow, so the risk is limited to user misuse.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution locations are Markdown code fences and Java SDK snippets. No executable command runner, dynamic shell invocation, or user-controlled command construction was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line review confirms these are fenced documentation examples, not runtime code in the skill. There is no shell execution primitive in SKILL.md.

dangerous_patterns.title: Networked Azure SDK Usage
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill guides users to call Azure Anomaly Detector service methods and use Azure Blob Storage data sources. This network behavior is core functionality and not evidence of exfiltration.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly perform Azure service calls and reference Azure endpoints. The destinations are standard Azure resources, not suspicious third-party endpoints.

content.user_title: Build Azure Anomaly Detection in Java
content.value_statement: Java teams need reliable examples for adding anomaly detection to time-series applications. This skill provides Azure SDK patterns for univariate, multivariate, streaming, and change point workflows.
actual_capabilities: Shows Maven dependency setup for the Azure AI Anomaly Detector Java SDK.
Demonstrates client creation with AzureKeyCredential and DefaultAzureCredential.
Explains univariate batch, streaming, and change point detection flows.
Provides multivariate model training, inference, and result inspection patterns.
Covers model listing, deletion, and basic HTTP error handling.
limitations: Uses SDK examples and does not provision Azure resources.
Requires a valid Azure Anomaly Detector endpoint and credentials.
Uses beta SDK version examples that may change over time.
Does not include a complete production monitoring application.

use_cases.title: Monitor Application Metrics
use_cases.description: Add anomaly detection to Java services that track latency, traffic, or error-rate time series.

use_cases.title: Analyze Operational Signals
use_cases.description: Build multivariate analysis for correlated machine, sensor, or business metrics stored in Azure.

use_cases.title: Prototype Alerting Logic
use_cases.description: Test batch and last-point anomaly detection before integrating alerts into production workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Client
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to create Azure AI Anomaly Detector Java clients using environment variables for endpoint and key.

prompt_templates.title: Detect Single-Series Anomalies
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me implement univariate batch anomaly detection for a daily Java time series and explain the result fields.

prompt_templates.title: Train a Multivariate Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through training a multivariate anomaly detection model from Azure Blob Storage data in Java.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Monitoring Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Java monitoring workflow that uses last-point detection, contributor analysis, error handling, and secure credential configuration.

output_examples.input: I need to detect spikes in a daily revenue time series.
output_examples.output: A concise Java workflow using univariate batch detection, daily granularity, sensitivity tuning, and result interpretation.

output_examples.input: I have correlated sensor streams in Azure Blob Storage.
output_examples.output: A multivariate training and inference plan that references blob data, model status checks, and top contributor review.

output_examples.input: I want streaming anomaly alerts for the latest metric value.
output_examples.output: A last-point detection approach with expected value ranges, anomaly checks, and HTTP error handling guidance.

best_practices: Store Azure keys and SAS URLs in secret management systems, not source files.
Match time granularity to the actual sampling frequency before detection.
Tune sensitivity and sliding windows with representative historical data.
anti_patterns: Do not commit real API keys, endpoints with secrets, or SAS URLs.
Do not train multivariate models on short or poorly aligned signal windows.
Do not ignore service errors or model status during long-running operations.

faq.question: Does this skill install the Azure SDK?
faq.answer: No. It shows the Maven dependency and usage patterns for a Java project.

faq.question: Does it support both univariate and multivariate detection?
faq.answer: Yes. The guide includes examples for both single-series and correlated-signal workflows.

faq.question: Can I use managed identity instead of API keys?
faq.answer: Yes. The guide includes a DefaultAzureCredential client creation example.

faq.question: Does it create Azure resources for me?
faq.answer: No. You must provide an existing Azure endpoint, credentials, and data source.

faq.question: Is this safe to use with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes, with normal care for secrets and networked Azure service calls.

faq.question: What data format does multivariate training use?
faq.answer: The examples assume zipped time-series data available through Azure Blob Storage.
